docs.title: Sanctions and Export Controls — AI, Advanced Computing & Oil/Energy
docs.text:
A curated resource on US export controls and sanctions as applied to artificial intelligence, advanced computing, and the oil/energy sector.

Maintained by Felipe Villasuso Lago (AIQ / London South Bank University — LSBU).

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
This repository collects primary regulatory sources, law-firm analysis, enforcement guidance, and weekly news digests covering:

  • Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) Export Administration Regulations (EAR)
  • OFAC sanctions programmes and the 50% / UBO aggregation rule
  • AI Diffusion Rule and advanced-computing export controls
  • BIS Affiliates Rule (snaps back 10 November 2026)
  • Encryption controls and open-source software scope
  • Extraterritorial jurisdiction and enforcement
  • Sanctions exposure in the oil, gas & energy sector

docs.title: Repository Structure
docs.text:
regulations/
  primary-sources.md             — EAR, AI Diffusion Rule, OFAC, DOJ NSD
  ai-advanced-tech-export-controls.md
  extraterritorial-enforcement.md
  encryption-open-source.md
  ofac-ubo-50-percent-rule.md
  bis-affiliates-rule.md
  sanctions-oil-energy.md
news/
  digest.md                      — weekly auto-updated digest
.github/workflows/
  weekly-update.yml              — Monday 08:00 UTC scraper workflow
mkdocs.yml                       — GitHub Pages (MkDocs) configuration
CITATION.cff

docs.title: Related Repositories

docs.title: Documentation Site
docs.text:
This repository is published via MkDocs + GitHub Pages. After enabling Pages in repository settings (source: gh-pages branch), the site will be available at:

https://felipelago17.github.io/Sanctions-and-Export-Controls-AI-and-Oil/

docs.title: Citation
docs.text: See CITATION.cff for formal citation metadata.

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text: This resource is for informational and academic purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. Always consult qualified export-control and sanctions counsel before taking compliance decisions.

docs.title: Additional Sources & Literature
docs.text: A curated index of publications, trackers, academic literature, and monitoring services relevant to US export controls, sanctions, AI governance, and the energy sector. These sources are monitored by the daily digest workflow.

docs.title: United States
docs.text: Source URL What to monitor BIS Press Releases https://www.bis.gov/press-release Entity List updates, new rules, enforcement actions BIS Federal Register filings https://www.federalregister.gov/agencies/bureau-of-industry-and-security Proposed and final rules OFAC Press Releases https://ofac.treasury.gov/press-releases Designations, general licences, enforcement OFAC Sanctions List (SDN) https://sanctionssearch.ofac.treas.gov/ Daily SDN/SSI/FSE list changes OFAC Recent Actions RSS https://ofac.treasury.gov/recent-actions Programme-level updates Treasury Press Releases https://home.treasury.gov/news/press-releases Interagency sanctions coordination DOJ NSD Export Controls https://www.justice.gov/nsd/export-control-and-sanctions Criminal enforcement, charging documents State Dept DDTC (ITAR) https://www.pmddtc.state.gov/ ITAR/USML regulatory updates Federal Register — Commerce https://www.federalregister.gov/agencies/commerce-department Parent department notices BIS "Don't Let This Happen to You" https://www.bis.gov/compliance-a-training/export-management-a-compliance/dont-let-happen-you Enforcement case studies — essential reading Consolidated Screening List https://www.trade.gov/consolidated-screening-list Cross-agency restricted party list

docs.title: Regulatory Visuals & Decision Tools
docs.text: Interactive diagrams for navigating US export controls and sanctions frameworks. All diagrams are rendered in-browser via Mermaid.

docs.title: Regulatory Timeline: AI & Energy Sanctions (2022–2026)
docs.text:
timeline
    title Key US Export Controls & Sanctions Events
    section 2022
        Oct 7 : BIS Advanced Computing Rule
              : First GPU/chip ECCN controls (3A090)
              : Entity List — Yangtze Memory, CXMT
    section 2023
        Oct 17 : "October 17" Rule Update
               : Expanded chip controls, updated TPP thresholds
               : Huawei Mate 60 Pro triggers review
    section 2024
        Jan–Dec : AI governance debates intensify
                : Proposed AI Diffusion Rule published Dec 2024
    section 2025
        Jan 15 : AI Diffusion Rule — Interim Final Rule
               : Three-tier country framework enacted
               : New ECCNs for AI model weights
        May    : AI Diffusion Rule officially paused
               : BIS elevates compliance guidance regardless
        Oct    : BIS Affiliates Rule enacted
               : 50% Rule extended to Entity List & MEU List
    section 2026
        Jun    : Repository established — AIQ / LSBU
        Nov 10 : ⚠️ BIS Affiliates Rule snap-back date

docs.title: AI Hardware Export: Compliance Decision Tree
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A([AI Hardware / Software / Technology]) --> B{Subject to EAR?}
    B -- "Open source,\npublicly available,\nno US-person control" --> C([✅ Not subject to EAR])
    B -- Yes --> D{Determine ECCN}
    D -- EAR99 --> C
    D -- "3A090\nAdvanced chips\n≥ TPP threshold" --> E{Destination country tier?}
    D -- "5E002\nEncryption technology" --> F{Mass-market eligible?}
    D -- "4E001 / 5D002\nSoftware" --> G{End-use / end-user check}
    E -- "Tier 1\nUS allies" --> H([✅ Licence exception\nauthorized — report])
    E -- "Tier 2\nGeneral commercial" --> I{Data Centre\nValidated End User?}
    E -- "Tier 3\nEmbargo / restricted" --> J([❌ Licence required\nPolicy of denial])
    I -- Yes DCVEU --> H
    I -- No --> K([📋 Licence application\nrequired])
    F -- Yes --> L([✅ Section 740.17 ENC\nAnnual reporting])
    F -- No --> M([📋 ENC review\nor licence needed])
    G --> N{Entity List\nMEU List\nUVL check?}
    N -- Listed or\naffiliate of listed --> J
    N -- Clean --> O([✅ Proceed with\nstandard due diligence])

    style C fill:#16a34a,color:#fff
    style H fill:#16a34a,color:#fff
    style L fill:#16a34a,color:#fff
    style O fill:#16a34a,color:#fff
    style J fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style K fill:#d97706,color:#fff
    style M fill:#d97706,color:#fff

docs.title: OFAC 50% Rule: Ownership Aggregation Chains
docs.text:
The diagram below illustrates how SDN restrictions flow through ownership structures — including cases where no single SDN holds ≥ 50% alone.

graph TD
    SDN1["🔴 SDN Entity A\n(e.g. sanctioned bank)"]
    SDN2["🔴 SDN Entity B\n(e.g. state oil company)"]

    SDN1 -->|"owns 60%"| Sub1["⛔ Subsidiary X\nBLOCKED — 60% ≥ 50%"]
    SDN1 -->|"owns 30%"| JV["Joint Venture Co."]
    SDN2 -->|"owns 25%"| JV
    JV   -->|"30% + 25% = 55%\naggregate ≥ 50%"| JV_BLOCKED["⛔ Joint Venture Co.\nBLOCKED — aggregate rule"]
    Sub1 -->|"owns 80%"| GC["⛔ Grandchild Entity\nBLOCKED — indirect chain"]
    SDN1 -->|"owns 40%"| Safe["✅ Holding Co.\nNOT blocked — 40% < 50%\n(verify no other SDN stake)"]

    style SDN1 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style SDN2 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style Sub1 fill:#ef4444,color:#fff
    style JV_BLOCKED fill:#ef4444,color:#fff
    style GC fill:#ef4444,color:#fff
    style Safe fill:#16a34a,color:#fff
    style JV fill:#f97316,color:#fff

Key rule: Aggregation applies even where no single SDN holds ≥ 50% alone. Always trace full ownership chains and add up all SDN-held stakes. See OFAC FAQ 1521.

docs.title: BIS Affiliates Rule: Scope Diagram
docs.text:
graph LR
    EL["Entity List Party\nor MEU List Party"]
    UVL["Unverified List\nor MIEU Party"]

    EL -->|"owns ≥ 50%\n(direct or indirect)"| AF1["⛔ Affiliate A\nLicence required\n(policy of denial)"]
    EL -->|"aggregate ≥ 50%\nwith other EL parties"| AF2["⛔ Affiliate B\nAggregation applies"]
    UVL -->|"owns ≥ 50%"| AF3["⚠️ Affiliate C\nHeightened scrutiny"]
    AF1 -->|"owns ≥ 50%"| AF4["⛔ Sub-affiliate\nChain extends downstream"]

    SNAPBACK["⚠️ Snap-back:\n10 Nov 2026\nUnless extended"]

    style EL fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style UVL fill:#f97316,color:#fff
    style AF1 fill:#ef4444,color:#fff
    style AF2 fill:#ef4444,color:#fff
    style AF3 fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    style AF4 fill:#ef4444,color:#fff
    style SNAPBACK fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff

docs.title: Russian Oil Price Cap: Compliance Chain
docs.text:
graph TD
    Cargo["Russian Crude Oil\n(seaborne)"] --> Ship["Tanker\n(any flag)"]
    Ship --> Svc["Services Chain"]

    Svc --> Owner["Shipowner"]
    Svc --> Charter["Charterer"]
    Svc --> Broker["Broker"]
    Svc --> Insurer["P&I Club / Insurer"]
    Svc --> Bank["Trade Finance Bank"]

    Cap["G7 Price Cap\nUSD 60/bbl crude\nUSD 45/bbl fuel oil\nUSD 100/bbl premium"]

    Owner -->|"Must attest price\n≤ cap at sale"| Cap
    Charter --> Cap
    Broker --> Cap
    Insurer --> Cap
    Bank --> Cap

    Cap -->|"Price > cap\nor no attestation"| Sanction["⛔ SDN designation\nof vessel / operator\nOFAC enforcement"]
    Cap -->|"Price ≤ cap\n+ valid attestation"| OK["✅ Services\npermitted"]

    style Sanction fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style OK fill:#16a34a,color:#fff
    style Cap fill:#1d4ed8,color:#fff

Diagrams rendered with Mermaid. Source: docs/visuals.md.

docs.title: Daily Digest — Sanctions & Export Controls
docs.text:
Auto-updated every day at 07:00 UTC by .github/workflows/daily-update.yml.

Sources monitored: OFAC · BIS · DOJ NSD · Treasury · Federal Register · Baker McKenzie Sanctions News · CNAS · Lawfare · Just Security · Atlantic Council · RUSI · Chatham House · Carnegie Endowment · Brookings · Steptoe · Cleary Gottlieb · Gibson Dunn · WilmerHale · Covington · WorldECR

See Additional Sources & Literature for the full source index.

docs.title: AI & Advanced Technology Export Controls
docs.text: Analysis and commentary on BIS controls targeting advanced computing chips, AI model weights, and related software. The regulatory landscape has evolved rapidly since the October 2022 advanced-computing rules; the sources below cover the January 2025 AI Diffusion Rule and subsequent developments.

docs.title: Sidley Austin — New US Export Controls on Advanced Computing & AI Model Weights (Jan 2025)
docs.text:
URL: https://www.sidley.com/en/insights/newsupdates/2025/01/new-us-export-controls-on-advanced-computing-items-and-artificial-intelligence-model-weights

Comprehensive overview of the January 2025 Interim Final Rule. Covers the three-tier country framework, new ECCN classifications for AI model weights, the role of Data Center Validated End Users (DCVEUs), and implications for cloud providers and hyperscalers. Includes a comparison to the October 2022 and October 2023 rules.

docs.title: Baker McKenzie Sanctions News — BIS Interim Final Rule on AI & Advanced Computing ICs
docs.text:
URL: https://sanctionsnews.bakermckenzie.com/bis-issues-interim-final-rule-and-call-for-comments-on-new-export-controls-on-artificial-intelligence-and-advanced-computing-integrated-circuits/

Focus on the integrated-circuit controls component of the AI Diffusion Rule, including new ECCN 3A090 parameters and the Total Processing Performance (TPP) thresholds that determine licensing requirements. Useful for hardware manufacturers and chip designers.

docs.title: WilmerHale — BIS Issues Long-Awaited Export Controls on AI (Feb 2025)
docs.text:
URL: https://www.wilmerhale.com/en/insights/publications/20250205-bis-issues-long-awaited-export-controls-on-ai

Analysis of the policy rationale behind the AI Diffusion Rule and its interaction with existing EAR controls. Addresses the model-weight definition, the "publicly available" exclusion, and compliance programme expectations for AI developers.

docs.title: National Law Review — BIS Issues Four Key Updates: Advanced Computing & AI Export Controls
docs.text:
URL: https://natlawreview.com/article/bis-issues-four-key-updates-advanced-computing-and-ai-export-controls

Summary of four simultaneous regulatory updates accompanying the Diffusion Rule: (1) Entity List additions, (2) Military End-User (MEU) list expansion, (3) Validated End-User (VEU) programme changes, and (4) new Foreign Direct Product Rule (FDPR) provisions targeting AI supply chains.

docs.title: Mayer Brown — Commerce Announces New Export Compliance Expectations for AI (May 2025)
docs.text:
URL: https://www.mayerbrown.com/en/insights/publications/2025/05/us-commerce-department-announces-new-export-compliance-expectations-related-to-artificial-intelligence

Covers BIS guidance on compliance programme expectations specifically for AI-related exports: know-your-customer obligations for AI hardware distributors, due-diligence requirements for model-weight transfers, and red-flag indicators published by BIS for AI transactions.

Last reviewed: June 2026

docs.title: BIS Affiliates Rule & 50% Ownership Expansion
docs.text:
⚠ Snap-back date: 10 November 2026

The BIS Affiliates Rule, which extends Entity List and Military End-User (MEU) List controls to affiliates owned 50% or more by listed entities, is currently in effect. The current administration has signalled that the rule will automatically snap back on 10 November 2026 unless affirmatively extended. Compliance programmes should track this date closely.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The BIS Affiliates Rule mirrors OFAC's 50% Rule in the export-control context: it treats affiliates of Entity List or MEU List parties as themselves subject to licence requirements, even if the affiliate is not independently listed. This closes a significant compliance gap where controlled-party transactions were routed through unlisted subsidiaries or joint-venture vehicles.

The rule applies to: - Entities owned 50% or more by a single Entity List / MEU List party - Entities owned 50% or more in the aggregate by two or more such parties - Both direct and indirect ownership chains

docs.title: BIS Press Release — Commerce Expands Entity List to Cover Affiliates of Listed Entities
docs.text:
URL: https://www.bis.gov/press-release/department-commerce-expands-entity-list-cover-affiliates-listed-entities

Official BIS announcement of the Affiliates Rule. Describes the policy rationale (closing the "affiliate loophole"), the scope of covered entities, and the effective date. Contains the regulatory text and a summary of public comments received.

docs.title: White & Case — BIS Implements Affiliates Rule: 50% Rule Applicable to Entity List and Military End-User List
docs.text:
URL: https://www.whitecase.com/insight-alert/bis-implements-affiliates-rule-50-rule-applicable-entity-list-and-military-end-user

Detailed client alert on the mechanics of the BIS Affiliates Rule. Covers the ownership aggregation methodology, how to conduct an affiliate determination under the rule, interaction with existing Entity List licence requirements (generally a policy of denial), and recommended compliance programme updates for exporters with complex supply chains.

docs.title: Willkie Farr & Gallagher — BIS Brings the 50% Rule to Export Controls: Heightening Compliance Risks (Oct 2025)
docs.text:
URL: https://www.willkie.com/-/media/files/publications/2025/10/bis-brings-the-50-percent-rule-to-export-controls-heightening-compliance-risks.pdf

Analysis of how the BIS Affiliates Rule interacts with the OFAC 50% Rule to create overlapping and sometimes inconsistent compliance obligations. Discusses the snap-back provision, the interagency coordination implications, and practical due-diligence steps for multinational companies.

docs.title: Sullivan & Cromwell — BIS Establishes 50% Rule for Certain Restricted End-Users (Oct 2025)
docs.text:
URL: https://www.sullcrom.com/insights/memo/2025/October/BIS-Establishes-50-Percent-Rule-Certain-Restricted-End-Users

Legal memorandum covering the rule's application to Unverified List (UVL) parties and Military Intelligence End-Users (MIEUs) in addition to Entity List and MEU List parties. Addresses transitional guidance for existing contracts and the licence application process for transactions caught by the rule.

Last reviewed: June 2026

docs.title: Encryption Controls, Open Source & EAR Scope
docs.text: Encryption items have their own licensing and reporting framework under EAR Part 742.15 and Supplement No. 3. Many AI and software products incorporate encryption, making these rules directly relevant. Open-source software raises distinct questions about EAR applicability.

docs.title: eCFR — 15 C.F.R. § 742.15 (Encryption Controls)
docs.text:
URL: https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-15/subtitle-B/chapter-VII/subchapter-C/part-742/section-742.15

The primary regulatory text governing encryption export controls under the EAR. Covers ECCN 5E002 (encryption technology), the mass-market encryption exception, annual self-classification reporting requirements (via the Encryption Registration portal), and the review process for non-US government end-users.

docs.title: BIS — Encryption Items Not Subject to EAR
docs.text:
URL: https://media.bis.gov/learn-support/encryption-controls/encryption-items-not-subject-to-ear

Official BIS guidance identifying categories of encryption products that fall outside EAR jurisdiction entirely: publicly available software with no US-person involvement, certain open-source cryptographic libraries, and products subject only to EAR99 classification. Critical for open-source AI projects that incorporate cryptographic modules.

docs.title: Encryption Authority — US Export Controls on Encryption
docs.text:
URL: https://encryptionauthority.com/us-export-controls-on-encryption

Practitioner-oriented overview of the full encryption control framework: classification under ECCN 5x002, the Section 740.17 licence exceptions (ENC), mass-market eligibility criteria, and the annual reporting obligation. Useful companion to the eCFR text.

docs.title: Baker McKenzie — BIS Updates Reporting Requirements for Mass-Market Encryption & Publicly Available Software
docs.text:
URL: https://sanctionsnews.bakermckenzie.com/bis-updates-reporting-requirements-relating-to-mass-market-encryption-items-and-publicly-available-software-and-also-updates-certain-classifications/

Analysis of BIS amendments to annual self-classification reporting for mass-market encryption products. Covers how changes affect software-as-a-service (SaaS) providers and cloud platforms that deliver encryption functionality, and the interaction with the "publicly available" exclusion for open-source software.

Last reviewed: June 2026

docs.title: Extraterritorial Jurisdiction & Enforcement
docs.text: US export controls and sanctions have extensive extraterritorial reach through mechanisms like the Foreign Direct Product Rule (FDPR), the de minimis rule, and correspondent-banking pressure. This page collects resources on how BIS and OFAC enforce controls beyond US borders.

docs.title: Morrison Foerster — Managing Export Control Risks in the AI Chip Ecosystem (Feb 2026)
docs.text:
URL: https://www.mofo.com/resources/insights/260209-managing-export-control-risks-in-the-ai-chip-ecosystem

In-depth treatment of how the FDPR applies to AI chips produced outside the US using US-origin software or equipment. Covers extraterritorial reach over non-US chip fabs, the "substantial transformation" test, and practical compliance steps for foreign chip manufacturers and distributors. Includes a discussion of third-country transshipment risks.

docs.title: BIS Enforcement — Official Enforcement Resources
docs.text:
URL: https://media.bis.gov/enforcement

BIS Office of Export Enforcement (OEE) official portal. Includes charging letters, settlement agreements, denial orders, and the Consolidated Screening List. Key reading for understanding how BIS exercises enforcement discretion and what aggravating/mitigating factors it considers.

docs.title: WilmerHale — AI Diffusion Rule Officially Paused; New Guidance Elevates Risk for AI-Related Exports (May 2025)
docs.text:
URL: https://www.wilmerhale.com/en/insights/client-alerts/20250515-us-export-controls-on-ai-diffusion-officially-paused-but-new-guidance-elevates-risk-for-ai-related-exports

Despite the formal pause on the AI Diffusion Rule's implementation, BIS simultaneously issued compliance guidance that raises due-diligence expectations for all AI-related exports regardless of the rule's status. This alert explains why the pause does not reduce compliance risk and what BIS expects of exporters in the interim period.

Last reviewed: June 2026

docs.title: OFAC UBO Aggregation & 50% Rule
docs.text:
OFAC's 50% Rule is one of the most consequential and least well-understood rules in US sanctions compliance. It extends SDN-equivalent restrictions to any entity owned 50% or more (individually or in aggregate) by one or more SDN-listed parties — even if that entity does not itself appear on the SDN List.

See also: Felipe Villasuso Lago, "The Affiliate Trap: OFAC's 50% Rule and Aggregation Risk in Joint Ventures", Oxford Business Law Blog (OBLB), 2026. The article analyses how the aggregation mechanic creates hidden sanctions exposure in multi-party joint ventures and private equity structures, with particular focus on the oil & gas sector.

docs.title: OFAC FAQ — Topic 1521: The 50% Rule
docs.text:
URL: https://ofac.treasury.gov/faqs/topic/1521

The authoritative OFAC guidance on the 50% Rule. Explains that US persons are prohibited from dealing with entities owned 50% or more by one or more SDN-designated parties, even absent a separate SDN designation of the entity. Addresses aggregation across multiple SDN owners, indirect ownership chains, and the interaction with sectoral sanctions.

Key points from the FAQ: - Ownership is assessed at the time of the transaction - Aggregation applies even where no single SDN owns 50%+ alone - The rule applies to all OFAC sanctions programmes unless a specific programme states otherwise - OFAC does not maintain a comprehensive list of blocked entities under the 50% Rule — parties must conduct their own due diligence

docs.title: Sanctionfy — OFAC 50% Rule (2026 Commentary)
docs.text:
URL: https://www.sanctionfy.com/blog/2026/ofac-50-percent-rule

Current practitioner commentary on OFAC's 50% Rule, covering recent enforcement actions that turned on UBO aggregation issues, evolving best-practice approaches for beneficial ownership screening, and the interaction of the 50% Rule with FinCEN's Corporate Transparency Act (CTA) beneficial ownership database.

Last reviewed: June 2026

docs.title: Primary Regulations & Official Sources
docs.text: Authoritative primary sources for US export controls and sanctions. These are the starting point for any compliance analysis.

docs.title: Export Administration Regulations (EAR) — Full Text
docs.text:
URL: https://media.bis.gov/regulations/ear

The complete codified text of the EAR (15 C.F.R. Parts 730–774), administered by the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS), US Department of Commerce. The EAR controls exports, re-exports, and in-country transfers of dual-use goods, software, and technology. Key parts include Part 734 (scope), Part 738 (CCL), Part 742 (controls for specific reasons), Part 744 (end-user/end-use controls), and Part 774 (Commerce Control List with ECCNs).

docs.title: AI Diffusion Rule — Federal Register
docs.text:
URL: https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2025/01/15/2025-00636/framework-for-artificial-intelligence-diffusion

The January 2025 Interim Final Rule establishing a tiered framework for exports of advanced AI model weights and associated computing infrastructure. Creates three tiers of countries with differentiated licensing requirements. Note: implementation has been paused pending further rulemaking — see Extraterritorial Enforcement.

docs.title: OFAC Homepage
docs.text:
URL: https://ofac.treasury.gov

The Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC), US Department of the Treasury. Administers and enforces economic and trade sanctions based on US foreign policy and national security goals against targeted foreign countries, regimes, terrorists, proliferators, narcotics traffickers, and other threats. Primary portal for SDN list searches, programme information, licences, and FAQs.

docs.title: OFAC Sanctions Programs & Country Information
docs.text:
URL: https://ofac.treasury.gov/sanctions-programs-and-country-information

Consolidated index of all active OFAC sanctions programmes, including comprehensive (country-based) and list-based (targeted) programmes. Relevant energy-sector programmes include Russia-related (RUSAGG), Iran (IFSR), Venezuela (JCGC), and Sudan.

docs.title: DOJ National Security Division — Export Controls & Sanctions
docs.text:
URL: https://www.justice.gov/nsd/export-control-and-sanctions

The Department of Justice, National Security Division (NSD) coordinates federal criminal enforcement of export control and sanctions laws. Resources include enforcement priority statements, charging guidance, and major case summaries. Criminal penalties under the Export Control Reform Act (ECRA) and the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) are enforced here.

Last reviewed: June 2026

docs.title: Sanctions Programs, Enforcement & Oil/Energy Sector
docs.text: The oil, gas, and broader energy sector sits at the intersection of many of the most significant US sanctions programmes. Russia, Iran, Venezuela, and Sudan are the primary exposure jurisdictions; the Russia-related programmes have expanded most rapidly since 2022 and now include sector-specific and price-cap measures that affect tanker operators, trading firms, insurers, and financiers globally.

docs.title: OFAC — Industry Groups Information
docs.text:
URL: https://ofac.treasury.gov/additional-ofac-resources/ofac-information-for-industry-groups

OFAC's dedicated guidance portal for specific industry sectors, including energy and petroleum, financial services, maritime shipping, and insurance. Contains sector-specific FAQs, guidance documents, and links to relevant general licences. The energy-sector page addresses the Russian oil price cap mechanism and related compliance expectations for operators in the shipping and financing chain.

docs.title: OFAC Press Releases
docs.text:
URL: https://ofac.treasury.gov/press-releases

Official press releases for all OFAC designations, general licence issuances, enforcement actions, and guidance updates. Subscribe to the RSS feed or monitor this page for real-time sanctions programme changes. Energy-sector relevant releases include Russia energy-related designations, Iran oil-related actions, and Venezuela sectoral updates.

docs.title: OFAC SDN & Sanctions List Search
docs.text:
URL: https://sanctionssearch.ofac.treas.gov/

The official OFAC consolidated sanctions list search tool. Searches the Specially Designated Nationals (SDN) List, Sectoral Sanctions Identifications (SSI) List, Foreign Sanctions Evaders (FSE) List, and other OFAC-maintained lists simultaneously. Essential for counterparty screening; note that the 50% Rule (see OFAC UBO & 50% Rule) means list-search alone is insufficient — UBO analysis is also required.

docs.title: Key Energy-Sector Sanctions Programmes
docs.text: Programme Scope Key Regulation RUSAGG (Russia) Energy, finance, defence, tech E.O. 14024; 31 C.F.R. Part 587 IFSR (Iran) Oil, gas, petrochemicals, shipping 31 C.F.R. Part 560 JCGC (Venezuela) Oil sector, PDVSA E.O. 13850; 31 C.F.R. Part 591 Sudan / SDN Oil infrastructure 31 C.F.R. Part 538 Russia Oil Price Cap Tankers, traders, insurers G7 Price Cap Coalition

docs.title: Russian Oil Price Cap
docs.text:
The G7 price cap on Russian seaborne crude (USD 60/barrel) and refined products imposes compliance obligations on all service providers in the tanker supply chain — shipowners, charterers, brokers, insurers, and financial institutions — regardless of nationality. OFAC enforces the cap through its existing Russia-related programmes. Non-compliance can result in SDN designation of vessels and operators.

Last reviewed: June 2026
